title: IIFYM Macros Calculator - Flexible Diet | Hacé Cuentas
h1: IIFYM Macros Calculator: Flexible Diet Approach
description: Calculate your IIFYM macros for flexible dieting: 30% protein, 30% fat, 40% carbs. 80/20 rule: 80% nutritious, 20% flexible. Free calculator.
intro: **IIFYM (If It Fits Your Macros)**, also known as 'flexible dieting', is an approach to tracking macros without restricting specific foods: if it fits your daily numbers, you can eat it. Originating in the fitness community through Layne Norton, it works via [caloric deficit](/calculadora-deficit-calorico-perder-peso-semana) and adequate protein. Typical distribution is **30% protein / 30% fat / 40% carbs** for body recomposition, adjustable based on goals. The **80/20 rule** states that 80% of your intake should be nutrient-dense (protein, vegetables, whole grains) and 20% can be flexible (ice cream, pizza, chocolate). This calculator shows your target macros and how much 'room' you have for indulgences within the 80/20 framework. Popular with athletes and fitness enthusiasts.
keyTakeaway: IIFYM: **30% protein + 30% fat + 40% carbs**. 80/20 nutritious/flexible. If it fits, you eat it.

example.title: Example: 2500 calorie IIFYM breakdown
steps: **Flexible 20%**: 500 kcal for indulgences like ice cream or pizza.

explanation:
## What is IIFYM

**If It Fits Your Macros**. Track macros, not food rules.

## Standard macro ratios

| Macro | % | 2500 kcal |
|---|---|---|
| Protein | 30% | 188 g |
| Fat | 30% | 83 g |
| Carbs | 40% | 250 g |

## The 80/20 rule

- **80% nutrient-dense**: vegetables, fruits, whole grains, lean protein.
- **20% flexible**: eat what you want if it fits your macros.

## Pros

- High adherence and sustainability.
- Accommodates social eating.
- Backed by science: caloric deficit + adequate protein.
- No food groups forbidden.

## Cons

- Requires app and food scale.
- 'Empty calories' temptation.
- Doesn't prioritize micronutrients.
- Learning curve for tracking.

## Macro adjustment by goal

| Goal | Protein | Fat | Carbs |
|---|---|---|---|
| Muscle gain | 25% | 25% | 50% |
| Body recomposition | 30% | 30% | 40% |
| Fat loss | 40% | 30% | 30% |

## Common mistakes

1. Treating 20% 'flexible' as ultra-processed junk food.
2. Ignoring micronutrients entirely.
3. Inconsistent food tracking.

## Related tools

- [Protein per meal calculator](/calculadora-proteina-por-comida-anabolismo).
- [Daily fiber intake calculator](/calculadora-fibra-diaria-edad-sexo).
- [Nutrient density calculator](/calculadora-densidad-nutricional).

Consult a sports dietitian or nutrition coach for personalized guidance.

faq.a: If It Fits Your Macros. A flexible dieting approach where you track protein, fat, and carbs rather than restricting specific foods.

faq.q: Is IIFYM the same as counting calories?
faq.a: Similar but different. IIFYM tracks macros separately; counting calories is just total energy. IIFYM gives more nutritional control.

faq.q: Can I eat pizza on IIFYM?
faq.a: Yes, if it fits your daily macros. Pizza counts as calories and macros—budget it into your daily total and enjoy it guilt-free.
80% of meals should be nutrient-dense (vegetables, lean protein, whole grains); 20% can be foods you enjoy (desserts, fried foods, etc.).

faq.q: Do I need to track micronutrients on IIFYM?
faq.a: Not required, but recommended. Following the 80/20 rule naturally ensures good micronutrient intake.

faq.q: What apps are best for IIFYM tracking?
faq.a: Popular choices: MyFitnessPal, MacroFactor, Cronometer, and Carb Manager. Most have extensive food databases.

faq.q: Is IIFYM good for weight loss?
faq.a: Yes—it works through caloric deficit. The macro structure with 30-40% protein helps preserve muscle while losing fat.

faq.q: How accurate do I need to be with IIFYM?
faq.a: Within 5-10 grams per day is ideal, but perfect accuracy isn't necessary. Consistent tracking over weeks matters more than perfection.
Yes, but it has a learning curve. Start with a tracking app and a food scale; most people adjust within 2-3 weeks.

sources.name: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health
